core_identity.title: Solving Quadratic Equations by Completing the Square
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: solving quadratic equations by completing the square (vertex form)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Rewrite quadratics in the form a(x + p)^2 + q by completing the square, then solve quadratic equations (including those with irrational roots) and explain why the method works as a geometric and algebraic transformation. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: What if we could turn any quadratic into a perfect square?

knowing_world.focus: Completing the square rewrites a quadratic into vertex form a(x + p)^2 + q, making it possible to solve equations even when factorising is not possible and helping reveal the vertex of a parabola.
key_concepts: Rewrite ax^2 + bx + c as a(x + p)^2 + q
Works when factorising doesn’t (including non-monic and irrational roots)
Vertex form (turning point) of a parabola
Add and subtract the same value to keep equality

human_story.focus: Completing the square dates back to ancient Babylon and was later formalised by mathematicians such as Al-Khwarizmi, creating a reliable method for solving quadratics long before modern calculators.

expressive_self.focus: Use visuals, dialogue, or teaching to communicate why the method works and to make the step sequence memorable.
activities: Use a square diagram to explain why the method works visually.
Write a dialogue between two students solving x^2 + 10x + c in different ways.
Record a short explanation teaching the steps to someone younger.

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive visual tool to manipulate coefficients and complete the square step by step, observing how the geometric square forms and how the algebra changes with b and c.
design_challenges: Start with Level 1: Use sliders to manipulate b and c in x^2 + bx + c.
Observe how the square is formed geometrically.
Try Level 2 or Level 3 with more challenging examples, possibly with irrational roots.

regulation_supports: Form a human square with four people as the sides, then “complete” the square by adding a small square piece to balance the edges while explaining the steps.

pathways.name: Final Task - Solve by Completing the Square
pathways.focus: Solve three quadratic equations by completing the square, including one that results in irrational roots, and connect the method to a real-world scenario.
products: Full working for three completed-square solutions
At least one example with irrational roots
A short explanation of why the added/subtracted value completes the square
One real-world scenario where completing the square could apply
assessment_criteria: Understanding: Shows the full process of completing the square accurately
Reasoning: Explains why specific values are added and subtracted
Application: Identifies and explains a meaningful real-world use

required: Solving linear equations using inverse operations
Comfort with rearranging expressions and working with halves/quarters
Basic understanding of quadratic expressions and equations

builds_toward: Quadratic formula (derived from completing the square)
Vertex form and sketching quadratic graphs
